speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three, the interview with Henry Wiener. The date is February 4, 1996. So you were just about to tell me something. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because many were sent to Mauthausen, there were many barracks empty at that time. One night, we wake up-- a tremendous explosion. What happened? A cockpit of a plane fell down onto the barracks and went on fire. What happened is that one of the pilots was shot down-- Australian pilot was shot down over Krakow by anti-aircraft fire. And he was still in the cockpit when he was-- fell down on one of the barracks, and it went on fire. The whole area went on fire. And eventually, we have-- we were sent to Plaszow, back to-- not to live there, but to sleep there. And we were walking back to work in Emalia, back and forth, because everything burned down. And in-- this was in 1994-- in-- sometimes in November before we were given orders. The Russian were approaching. The-- Schindler was-- said that he's moving the factory to Brunnlitz. And we were assigned-- our group stopped working on the construction. We were assigned to pack everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you tell me about leaving Plaszow's-- Schindler's camp, tell me a little bit more about that camp. What kind of-- you were building the barracks as you went along? This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in where? In Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in-- no, in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind--

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks were built.

speaker: interviewer
text: What of construction work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't want to say that, but we were digging graves.

speaker: interviewer
text: For whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Digging graves-- not graves. Digging stones. And the German master was taking out the corpses and pulling teeth-- pulling gold teeth from the corpses.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is while you were working for Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were--

speaker: interviewee
text: --while I was working in-- in Plaszow, before Schindler--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --before Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and we saw Jewish girls that were stripped naked and shot over there. They brought them from-- from the ghetto, from Krakow itself, because they were sort of on Aryan papers. In other words, they-- they posed as Poles. And they were pointed by somebody that they were Jewesses. And they were stripped naked over there and killed. And we had to bury them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when that took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It must have been '43 or '44-- something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many girls were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were these girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Young girls-- young girls in their teens or 20s-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --blonde. They looked like Polish girls-- all of them. It was horrible. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And we did all-- all other kinds of-- kinds of works, also-- mending a fence over there and some other things. And-- [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --when-- when somebody ran-- there were-- I forgot to tell you. The third time in Bochnia, they run away-- somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took one guy with me. I said, look. I don't care what happens. I'm not staying in the barrack, because they take 30 now, and they'll kill them. And I'm not going to stay. So I ran away with somebody else. It was a big coal mine depot on the premises of the camp itself. And we buried ourselves over there. I said, I'm not getting out until I hear shots. And after shots were fired, we came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what we looked for-- Schindler's at that time. I was-- hid. I was smacked, but I was not killed. They found out that I wasn't the-- at the appell when they called-- he called my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I gave him some story that I couldn't take it. I had to go to bathroom, or-- whatever it was. But this was prior to Schindler. Then we make up our minds. Let's-- let's get out of there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what-- this-- this was-- there was also an instance in-- in the beginning-- Plaszow, were there-- the girls were humming some Jewish song or Polish songs-- whatever it is. Some German heard. He said, for doing this-- and they made a public hanging of the two girls-- public hanging. Everybody had to stay, and everybody had to look at it. He said, somebody who doesn't look at it will be shot. The police-- not the police-- the soldiers was all around and watching anybody who didn't. This was when we came in the beginning in-- into this. In ghetto, I was doing everything. He was-- he was shooting anybody just without provocation. Don't ever-- he doesn't-- didn't need it. And actually, we were like, life-- life commodity, so to speak. He probably shouldn't have bought us from him or paid him for it-- whatever it is. But it meant to be this way. So eventually, we-- one week he went-- we went-- before we went to Schindler, we loaded on the railroad cars. He had two beautiful cars. I think it was a-- one was a Mercedes, and one was some other foreign car. We put ammunition boxes around it. You shouldn't be able to see it. That's how we send them all to Brunnlitz, because he also didn't want the Germans should see what he's doing. But over there, we build-- in Brunnlitz itself-- we built the barracks. We built--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm still not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before we go to Brunnlitz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --I wanted to spend a little more time in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me that some of the construction work had been to excavate graves that the Germans had filled.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excavate graves and pull out the skeletons. I knew they were pulling teeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does the-- and the--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they used the-- the-- the stones-- they used for-- for other purposes.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many bodies did you deal with in that-- in that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were working there for about four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were many people buried there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, of course. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you estimate how many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't estimate. I wouldn't estimate. It's hard to estimate.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But you worked-- you know, you work weeks over there, and then change of some-- to some-- to some place else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went to Schindler's camp in Plaszow, what kind of construction work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were building part of a factory itself, adjacent to the factory. We were building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like in Schindler's place in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: You worked. You were fed a little bit better-- not that much better, but fed better. But the-- the fear of death was not there. See, that's-- this was the main thing. Whereby in-- in Plaszow, you're not aware-- you're not sure will you come back alive, because of this-- of Get. But by-- by Schindler, you-- you were not-- the fear of death was not there. Not that you had more food or you had other things, but the fear was not there. This was the main thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get-- did people move around freely? Did you have a sense of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were in barracks. The whole thing was in the barracks done. You had to have somebody you know in the barracks in order to talk to him, to do anything. I-- some we have. Where I belong now to [NON-ENGLISH] Society, you know, I belong to the New French Krakow Society, where mostly, a lot of them who were by Schindler belonged there, and a few of them that I was with them in-- in a [NON-ENGLISH] by Schindler-- a few of them. Some I don't remember, and some do. It all depends upon the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you interact with Mr. Schindler a great deal?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had nothing to-- he was-- he only talked to the higher echelon. I was-- I was-- [LAUGHS] --19 maybe at that time or something. But he was passing by. He didn't ask me exactly. He asked our foreman, look, how are you doing here, and all the other things, you know. I mean, he-- you know, like anybody-- human being is asking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when you went to Brunnlitz, how did you go to Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Brunnlitz, we didn't go directly. We went to Brunnlitz-- first we stepped in-- sort of stepped in, quote, unquote, to Gross-Rosen concentration camp. Because this was the main headquarters of the area where Brunnlitz belonged to. And if you want to use the word hell on earth, this was a place. It was hell on earth. They didn't let us do anything there. They gave us breakfast about 2 o'clock in the morning. They gave us lunch about 7 o'clock in the morning. And by 10 o'clock, you think you were done. And you didn't do anything. You slept this way. That's how you had to sleep-- really. You couldn't stretch yourself out. But thank God we were not there too long. We were there about five or six days, if I remember correctly. Because they knew that we are only temporary, so they gave us as much problems as possible. But we all-- nobody died from our group over there. But we saw atrocities over there-- unbelievable-- unbelievable. The other people that were there-- whatever we saw was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We-- we saw-- one person flipped. And I never saw it before-- really, in all the years that you would normally-- you would think that people will flip, really. I saw, over there, one guy flip-- completely flipped. He was talking irrationally-- entirely irrationally. And they took him away. I understand that they killed him eventually. They-- they killed him, because-- he wasn't from our group. He was from-- from different groups. And their-- their foremens over there was a lot of Poles, a lot of Germans. And they were sadists-- all of them-- very sadistics. They were-- they-- they, you know, they behaved over there, and we-- we couldn't wait. They stripped us, first of all, naked-- all completely naked. They gave us purposely clothing everybody don't belong to. If you are fat, they give you a small. If you are-- you are small, they gave you big. They gave you two different shoes to make-- everything to make it-- to make it awful for us-- what-- what-- but this is the worst-- this is not the worst thing, you know. This-- this is only-- because we were there only a certain period of time. The women were worse off, because the women were sent to Auschwitz. They were not sure whether they were gonna-- he'll be able to take them out from there or not. We knew that we are new. I mean, now I'm saying. He knew that we are only in transit over there. Eventually, we wound up in Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Did you see any other atrocities in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not where we saw, because we were close together. All our group was always together. And always, they give us problems as a group itself. But there was no killing involved in-- in-- in our group. Whatever we came-- we, all of us, wound up in Brunnlitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and when did you get to Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a week after we left Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? This was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it must-- it must have been end of '44. And we were in Brunnlitz. Then we-- we had to build our own barracks, our own bathroom facilities, our shower facilities-- all-- we did all ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- when the women came in eventually, they all-- everything was built already. And when they came in, none of them had hair. Everything they had shaven. Everything was shaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know, hair grows back. But if you look at a woman without hair, you know, at that time-- but, you know, by us it wasn't comparing to others really, you know. Schindler really treated us well, I would say, on the overall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- did things get done in Brunnlitz? Was there production going on in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. I belonged-- personally, I belonged to a group-- also 10 boys under supervision of a man named Baruk Presser, who happened to live in Australia now. And we were instrumental in setting up in the factory the ammunition machines-- the machines itself. Whether they produced or not, that's another matter. But we setting up ammunition-- the big ammunition machines in the factory itself. This was our job. And they-- I understand they never produced a shell that worked, eventually. But this was not our job. Our job was to set up the machines. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it so happened that my brother, Nathan Krieger, one day, we-- I was outside. And I see a group of 30 people coming in. And they was talking about-- and they said, well, Schindler needed 30 mechanics.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they called him in and said, mechanics? What for mechanics? They said, well, that's what it is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And among them was my-- my brother-in-law. I mean, not-- now he's my brother-in-law. But he was and another friend of his, Kitchler, who is-- they happen to be both in Florida now. Anyway, but he-- he came in. And when he came in, I says, Nathan. I says, I think God brought you here. Here you'll be alive-- whatever it is. Although, we were under supervision of SS, anyway. Yes, this had nothing to do with it-- not at all. Every camp was under supervision of-- of the SS. The only thing is that Schindler made a deal with them. Don't interfere in my camp. If we have any problems, I'll call you. And that's-- that's-- that's-- this was the main-- main thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the daily routines, did you encounter the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were there. They were there, but they-- they didn't interfere too much. We were told that the main leader of the SS built graves for us already. Digged graves for us is the proper word, not built-- dig graves for us. He says that sooner or later, they will shoot them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Schindler was also instrumental of sending him out-- replacing him and sending him to a Russian front or someplace-- whatever it was. But he replaced him. So what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- after that, we are-- we were working there. This was already spring-- spring or end of the-- end of the winter-- beginning of spring of '45. And you smell the end of the war in the air. You just smelled. You just won't know whether we will persevere-- whether we were not gonna be at the last minute, the victims of it. And-- and Schindler was going out of the way to different factories to bring us food-- more food, because the delivery of food was cut-- cut already to the bone. Not-- the Germans didn't have it, either that much, but we had-- they didn't have it. We had less. But he went out of his way to different factories to bring food, and eventually bring some other clothes, also. Because this was a textile area in-- in-- in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we-- we didn't have that much to eat. One day I-- I said to a friend of mine, I says, you know what? We have to do something. I says-- not-- not enough food. So I made believe that I'm a foreman, and I taking him. And we went to the SS-- [LAUGHS] --cellar! We take the-- some tomatoes from them. I put it in an ammunition box. I says, let's go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was almost in it, and I was caught. He said, what's that inside? It's ammunition. He says, open up.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you are taking potatoes-- our potatoes. You were gonna feed yourself. Count the potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I happened-- it was small potatoes. I counted about 50 or something. He says, for every potato, you get smacked on your behind. And I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I was. I was smacked on the behind for every potato. But it doesn't-- it didn't kill me, at least, whatever it was. It was almost the end of-- the end of the war. Well, at that time, Brunnlitz was-- comparing to everything else, it was sort of a heaven on earth in comparing to-- to others. But she was very instrumental. Mrs. Schindler should be mentioned. They arrived on the railroad track-- arrived a train. And you heard voices inside. You heard voices. And it was wintertime. So Schindler-- she said, look. Let's open up and find out. And the SS guy didn't want it. Anyway, he was bribed. Open up, and you'll find over there human beings-- skeletons-- most of them frozen to death, some of them alive. So open all the wagons. And she, more or less, built a field hospital. Those who were alived-- alive, she tried to nurse to life. Whoever died, died. Whoever-- a lot of them were alive. Schindler bought a plot on a cemetery there-- some area-- I call it the Jewish cemetery. And there was-- they were buried proper way-- some of our-- our sort of quote, unquote inmates-- over there from this area. They're called [NON-ENGLISH] at that time. And a lot of them were alive, and everything because of-- of Mrs. Schindler. Not that he was such a good husband, but as far as she was a-- and she's still alive, by the way, as far as I know. But she was instrumental in-- in saving many lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people die in-- in Brunnlitz aside from this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- if they-- if they died, it was natural death. It was not death due to violence, as far as I remember correctly. Because mostly, we-- mostly were young people. Otherwise, they wouldn't have come there in the first place. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened in Brunnlitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brunnlitz, they-- when the war came to an end-- was it May 8th-- if I'm not-- not mistaken-- May 8, 1945. We wake up in the morning one day, and there was nobody there. Because prior to this-- about two weeks before, we-- there were change of SS completely. SS was gone, and the regular German soldiers were there. And they were old soldiers in the 60s already. And they were the one who were guiding-- guarding us, so to speak. But, you know, so this was already all right. We were not afraid at that time that we'll die, because-- because SS is not there-- unless something unexpectedly will occur. At-- and when we wake up May the 8th, there was nobody there-- no soldiers-- no Schindler-- and nobody. We were just free-- all of a sudden-- free. And there-- and I remember they were telling me that with Schindler, when someone of the high echelon from our-- this, his closest associate with him-- on the contrary, they should not kill him. They-- they should be the witnesses what he did for us. And they want to go and say, forget about the Russians. Let's go to the American zone. The Russians-- they don't care who is who.

speaker: interviewer
text: So eventually, they reached American zone, and that's how he is alive also. He didn't disappear like Wallenberg by the Russians. And the first, I remember, came a-- the first allied-- allied soldier, I remember, was a Czech-- Czech partisan girl on a horse.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a-- she must have been 18 or 19 years old. She came. She want to see-- she says, you're all free. And then the Russian soldiers came in after her. And then we-- we were free.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the-- that's the question. What does it mean, you're free? You wanna go-- you go anywhere? What does it mean anywhere? Home-- they were think, what are you gonna go home for? You know-- you know, as in-- in my case, I-- I knew that I'm not-- that my family is not alive-- down deep. I hoped that my brother's alive. And I hoped that my son is alive. Because I knew that she was-- she was going to be hidden by a Polish farmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my friend and I-- this Abusz Reich and I-- we decided to stay another week. And because one of the Czech family adopted us sort of. He says, we'll feed you. Don't worry about it. Don't go nowhere else. We'll feed you gradually. You shouldn't get sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-in-law, who was-- was married already, he went earlier. He says, look. I have to look whether Tosia is alive-- his wife. So he and his friend went before us. We stood another week and said, we'll decide what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had a brother. He know that his parents are not alive, so he hoped his brother is alive. I hoped that my brother is alive and Sally is alive. So eventually, we went by train to-- to Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: It took a long time, but eventually, we made it. I came to Krakow. So nobody knew about my brother, but they told me that Sally is alive. They says--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you ask in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a committee at that time, a Jewish committee. And there was somebody who came-- who was from Sally's town. So he told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, look. Sally is alive. She's by this and this farmer. I said, what is she doing by the farmer? He says, it's a long story. So anyway, so not this-- that was the day after, I said to my friend-- I says, you know what? Let's go. He says, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: His parents happened-- he was a tailor, his father. So he gave a machine to a-- to another farmer. So he says, let me go there. Maybe he will give me back the machine so I have something to-- to sell to have some money. But I forgot to-- prior to that, Schindler-- before the war ended, every one of us-- everyone in the Brunnlitz camp, got material for a shirt, for a suit, and for a coat. Every one of us got the package. He went-- Schindler, prior to-- to end of the war-- and he took from factories around-- textile factories. And he was the one who gave every one of us a package, so when we-- the war ended, we could sell it and have some-- some start. That's a very-- it's very important for me to tell you this. That's what he did-- very.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now-- now you were going with-- with your friend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we're going back to Krakow and Sally. So I go, and we come to Wisnicz. And I meet Sally. In the meantime, my friend goes to a different village for his-- because I said, look. We meet here and here, this and this time in the afternoon. Everybody told us, don't sleep anywhere. It's dangerous to sleep anywhere. Don't. I said, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to Sally, you know. And actually, we don't-- I didn't know-- not I didn't know. I said, I-- I have to talk to her and see what's happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because sometimes it could be that in order for you to save, maybe she made some commitments. But you cannot blame anybody, you know, for this. It's a matter of life and death. Thank God she made-- no commitments were made. And she was there. I says, look. I don't know what's what. I just came, and I wanna see you. And we have a understanding. I say, as soon as I find myself somewhere, I says, I'll take you. She says, you know, it's not a matter that parents are here or somebody's here. It's not the normal life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So OK. So I left, and I wait for my friend. He didn't come. So I didn't-- I didn't want to wait anymore. And I went back to Krakow. I went back to Krakow. My friend never came back. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found the farmer killed him with an ax-- killed him over there. He never came back. Because he wanted the machine back-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --the Polish farmer. In the meantime, I-- I contacted my farmer who had-- we were hidden. And-- and my father left pretty much clothing, [NON-ENGLISH] and other things-- you know, [NON-ENGLISH], or this. So I wanted to have some-- see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, come over here. Sleep over. Tomorrow I'll give you. I says, OK. I'll be there. I never went. I never went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- after what I heard, I says, forget it. And I never-- I never contacted him or anything of the sort.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll pause here for a tape change.
